jokes.text: Dad, are you sure you're okay to drive at night? It's night?!
jokes.setup: Bart questions Grandpa's night driving ability
jokes.punchline: Grandpa doesn't even realize it's night
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Grandpa's obliviousness revealed with minimal setup. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Tommy Dorsey's All-White Orchestra... to a time when girls were girls and a Hershey bar was as thick as a phone book
jokes.punchline: Nostalgic specificity about candy bar thickness
jokes.explanation: Classic Grandpa nostalgia with absurd specificity. The Hershey bar detail is perfectly random yet believable.

jokes.text: I'm sick of this song! / I don't like this one either... too optimistic! Too slow! Too fast! No zazz! Too much zazz! More boogie! Less woogie! My back hurts! All my friends are dead!
jokes.punchline: Escalates from musical complaints to existential despair
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from trivial to profound. 'All my friends are dead' is a masterful punchline that recontextualizes everything.

jokes.text: Nice one. Thanks. Oh, no, we're gonna die.
jokes.punchline: Casual acknowledgment of impending doom
jokes.explanation: Good understatement humor. The casual 'thanks' before realizing danger is well-timed.

jokes.text: Eventually I'll die in your home, which'll hurt the resale value
jokes.punchline: Practical concern about death's impact on property value
jokes.explanation: Dark humor meets real estate pragmatism. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I was having the most wonderful dream. I always wake up just before the good part.
jokes.setup: Grandpa waking up from apparent unconsciousness
jokes.punchline: Treating near-death as interrupted pleasant dream
jokes.explanation: Classic Grandpa optimism about his near-death experience. Character-consistent but not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: The money helps us serve the community. We plant trees, pick up litter, cut up milk bones for old dogs... / It was rhetorical question. / And I used rhetoric in my answer?
jokes.setup: Lisa explains cookie money usage, Quimby says it was rhetorical
jokes.punchline: Lisa's confusion about having used rhetoric
jokes.explanation: Smart wordplay that shows Lisa's intelligence while missing the point. Good character work.

jokes.text: We spent all our money on that new slogan for Springfield.
jokes.setup: Mayor explaining where city money went
jokes.punchline: Wasteful spending on presumably terrible slogan
jokes.explanation: Standard government incompetence joke. Functional but predictable.

jokes.text: If you don't give me those cookies, I'll pass a law forcing your organization to accept gay scout leaders. / I would strongly support that measure.
jokes.setup: Quimby threatens Lisa with progressive policy
jokes.punchline: Lisa enthusiastically supports the 'threat'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Quimby's threat backfires because Lisa is progressive. Well-structured reversal.

jokes.text: do you mean really broke, or uh, 'O.J. Simpson broke'?
jokes.setup: Homer asking about the city being broke
jokes.punchline: Distinguishing between actual poverty and celebrity 'broke'
jokes.explanation: Timely cultural reference that distinguishes types of 'broke.' Good economy and clear setup/payoff.

jokes.text: I've been reduced to using this cheap novelty gavel. Order! Order! [gavel breaks]
jokes.punchline: Gavel immediately breaks when used
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy - sets up cheap gavel, pays off immediately. Predictable but effective.

jokes.text: So I say we roll up our sleeves, and fake a disaster to trick the federal government out of relief money.
jokes.setup: Homer's inspiring speech about Americans coming together
jokes.punchline: Coming together to commit fraud
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - patriotic rhetoric leading to criminal conspiracy. Perfect Homer logic and structure.

jokes.text: Rip off the federal government? Mmm, I have reservations... for dinner in five minutes.
jokes.setup: Moe seeming to have moral qualms
jokes.punchline: Actually talking about dinner reservations
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection with perfect economy. The pause makes it work perfectly.

jokes.text: Now, when exactly did the hurricane pass through your town? Er, uh, five minutes ago. You just missed it.
jokes.setup: FEMA official asking about hurricane timing
jokes.punchline: Claiming it happened five minutes ago
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the fake disaster setup. The 'you just missed it' is a nice touch.

jokes.text: And that's how a fake FEMA official scammed us out of $2,500. We are now worse than broke.
jokes.punchline: They got counter-scammed by a fake official
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - scammers get scammed. Good karmic comedy with nice structure.

jokes.text: Everyone from... Comic Book Guy to... Milhouse! No one shall be spared!
jokes.punchline: Including a child in the tax dragnet
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdity in including Milhouse. The dramatic pause before 'Milhouse' helps the timing.

jokes.text: Bunk with me tonight, Kermit / Bunk with me tonight... / Uh, really, Lurleen?
jokes.punchline: Kermit's uncomfortable reaction to suggestive lyrics
jokes.explanation: Mild sexual innuendo creating awkward moment. The setup works but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: Dad was Colonel Homer and he wore that awesome suit and Lurleen wanted to bunk his brains out!
jokes.punchline: Crude summary of adult situation
jokes.explanation: Good callback to previous episode with Bart's inappropriate directness. The 'bunk his brains out' is memorably crude.

jokes.text: Kids, marriage is like a car. Along the way it has its bumps and dings, and this country can't make one that lasts more than five years.
jokes.setup: Marge using car metaphor for marriage
jokes.punchline: Switching to criticism of American auto industry
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection that starts as marriage advice and becomes economic commentary. Good structure.

jokes.text: With penalties, interest and the celebrity resentment tax, that sum has grown to $12 million.
jokes.punchline: Including 'celebrity resentment tax'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant fake tax category that satirizes public attitudes toward celebrities. Very quotable concept.

jokes.text: I told her not to go into one of those.
jokes.punchline: Homer treating it literally as a physical spiral
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - literal interpretation of metaphor delivered matter-of-factly. Excellent economy and character work.

jokes.text: Not for any reward, but for the pleasure of destroying someone they once admired. Save some for me!
jokes.punchline: Crowd member wanting in on the destruction
jokes.explanation: Good dark commentary on public bloodlust. The 'save some for me' adds to the mob mentality.

jokes.text: Got her! / Krusty? / I just want to win one thing in my life! / It's not real. / Don't you dare ruin the moment!
jokes.punchline: His desperate need to win something, anything
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Krusty's pathetic desperation. His plea not to ruin the moment is genuinely touching and funny.

jokes.text: Look at those idiots. I bet Lurleen's a million miles away right now. / Hey, Homer.
jokes.setup: Homer mocking searchers, betting Lurleen is far away
jokes.punchline: Lurleen immediately appears behind him
jokes.explanation: Classic dramatic irony setup and immediate payoff. Predictable but well-executed timing.

jokes.text: Shh! I'm more wanted than the last petal on a bloomin' onion.
jokes.punchline: Comparing herself to popular restaurant appetizer
jokes.explanation: Good Southern-flavored metaphor that fits Lurleen's character. The blooming onion reference is unexpectedly specific and funny.

jokes.text: You live here? / Well, at least she's got a friend. / It's your turn to clean the toilet. / Where is it? / You're holding it.
jokes.punchline: Possum holding the toilet, revealing extreme poverty
jokes.explanation: Great visual escalation showing extreme poverty. The possum roommate adds absurdity to the sadness.

jokes.text: Oh, it's a traditional Southern recipe: ketchup, Coca-Cola, and fricasseed possum. Just kiddin'. It's actually chicken.
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's actually chicken after listing bizarre ingredients
jokes.explanation: Mild misdirection playing on Southern stereotypes. The 'just kiddin' saves it from being mean-spirited.

jokes.text: Confederate degenerate? Southern-fried succubus? Hee-haw ho?
jokes.punchline: Increasingly creative and offensive Southern insults
jokes.explanation: Creative escalation of insults, with 'Hee-haw ho' being particularly inventive. Good alliteration and rhythm.

jokes.text: Chief, you okay? / Yeah, yeah. / Why'd you jump off the roof? / I just wanted to be extraordinary.
jokes.setup: Wiggum falling from roof during arrest
jokes.punchline: His motivation was seeking extraordinariness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum moment - his simple desire to be extraordinary explains reckless behavior. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: Well, I thought if we acted like The People's Court, some day we'd be The People's Court. And, uh, well, a bailiff can dream, can't he? / No, he can't.
jokes.punchline: Judge's harsh rejection of bailiff's dreams
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with harsh but funny putdown. The judge's absoluteness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Did it go up your nose? Between your toes?
jokes.punchline: Specific, bizarre places money might have gone
jokes.explanation: Mild Homer-ism with his literal thinking about where money could go. The rhyme helps the rhythm.

jokes.text: Boy, you sure have married a lot of super-hunks.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's matter-of-fact assessment of their attractiveness
jokes.explanation: Mild joke playing on Lisa's directness. 'Super-hunks' is a funny word choice for her.

jokes.text: All right, I need you to get the rats outta the jukebox, and clean the vomit out of the pool table pockets, and then you got the job. / Oh, and you've gotta share your tips with Manuel, who's, uh, actually me with a fake moustache. Es good job. You should take it.
jokes.punchline: Revealing his transparent scam with fake accent
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from gross job to obvious scam. Moe's shamelessness and bad accent make it funnier.

jokes.text: Oh, and you've gotta share your tips with Manuel, who's, uh, actually me with a fake moustache. Es good job. You should take it.
jokes.punchline: Obvious scam involving fake identity
jokes.explanation: Good Moe scheming with transparent deception

jokes.text: Say, Lurleen, are you Jamaican? 'Cause 'Jamaican' me crazy. / That's sweet, but I'm not lookin' to date. / No, I'm glad you said that. 'Cause you reminded me that I'm not looking a David.
jokes.setup: Barney's terrible pickup line and rejection
jokes.punchline: Barney's confused response mixing up 'looking to date' with 'looking at David'
jokes.explanation: Classic Barney confusion and misunderstanding. The wordplay breakdown is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Hey, Lurleen, I'm going down to the rock quarry tonight to throw stones at the woodchucks. Want to come? / No. / That's okay. I understand. Someone's lookin' at a snake in her mailbox.
jokes.punchline: His understanding reaction about snake-related obligations
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd date invitation followed by even more absurd understanding. Very quotable weirdness.

jokes.text: She turned down Lenny and Carl? That's like someone who doesn't like hamburgers or hot dogs. What could make her hate men like that?
jokes.setup: Homer's confusion about Lurleen rejecting his friends
jokes.punchline: Comparing men to basic American foods
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic equating his friends with universally loved foods. Shows his simple worldview.

jokes.text: How could a man just abandon his family? By which I mean, what is the method he would use, and could anyone do it?
jokes.setup: Homer seeming to condemn abandoning family
jokes.punchline: Actually asking for practical instructions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark turn showing Homer's true interest. Perfect structure with the clarifying 'by which I mean' - very quotable.

jokes.text: I've talked to over 500 men named Lumpkin. Everyone at Lumpkin, Lumpkin and Rosenthal Associates, an Asian or Pacific Islander named Cho Lum Kin... Good luck in searchings!
jokes.punchline: Including people with similar-sounding names from different cultures
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Homer's literal-mindedness in searching. The broken English adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: Lurleen? I ain't seen my little girl in 30 years. She must be what, uh, 12, 13 by now?
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong age calculation
jokes.explanation: Shows deadbeat dad's complete disconnection from reality. Good character establishment with economy.

jokes.text: Oh, man. I better whiskey up these corn flakes. / She needs to see you right away! / Oh, man. I better heroin up this orange juice.
jokes.punchline: Escalating to heroin in orange juice
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation showing the character's substance abuse. The casual delivery makes it funnier and more disturbing.

jokes.text: Lurleen?! My goodness. Why, you're the spittin' image of your mother's hotter sister!
jokes.setup: Father seeing grown daughter for first time
jokes.punchline: Comparing her to aunt rather than mother
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing inappropriate priorities. The specificity of 'hotter sister' is funny and character-revealing.

jokes.text: Can you forgive me, Lurleen? The way that I immediately forgave myself?
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's already forgiven himself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing narcissistic selfishness disguised as remorse. Perfect economy and character integration.

jokes.text: I made that hug happen! Wait a minute. He's a deadbeat dad, and yet, he's getting as much love as me, a dad too lazy to run away!
jokes.punchline: Realizes he gets no credit despite being consistently present
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-aware Homer moment. 'Too lazy to run away' is perfect character description and explains his parenting style.

jokes.text: Bart, get me my suicide axe. / No suicide axe! / Later.
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual denial and Homer's acceptance
jokes.explanation: Dark humor made light by Homer's casual acceptance and Bart's matter-of-fact refusal. Good family dynamic.

jokes.text: You know what I like best about this T-shirt? I'll always be able to see your face. / Oh, well, you don't need a T-shirt for that, Tootsie Pop, 'cause I ain't ever gonna leave you again.
jokes.punchline: Promise from someone who abandoned her for 30 years
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup given his history. The audience knows this promise is meaningless, creating dramatic irony.

jokes.text: Just sit still in my lap! / What lap? / Yeah, all I see is a gut with knees!
jokes.setup: Father asking Lurleen to sit in his lap
jokes.punchline: Kids pointing out his obesity
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup with kids' brutal honesty. 'Gut with knees' is a memorable description.

jokes.text: Lurleen's daddy comes back after 30 years, and he's father of the year. Why can't I leave for 30 years? / Fine with me. Look, I packed you a bag. / it might be cold outside. / No problem. I bought you a hat.
jokes.setup: Homer's jealousy leading to threats to leave
jokes.punchline: Marge being overly helpful with his departure
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with Marge calling Homer's bluff. Her preparedness reveals how often he makes empty threats.

jokes.text: Oh, I see. It's reverse psychology. Well, in that case, I'm not going, and I'm not staying. And I'll cut my steak with a spoon, then take a walk on the ceiling.
jokes.punchline: His illogical counter-strategy involving impossible actions
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - recognizes the game but plays it completely wrong. The ceiling walking is delightfully absurd.

jokes.text: Well, in that case, I'm not going, and I'm not staying. And I'll cut my steak with a spoon, then take a walk on the ceiling.
jokes.punchline: Illogical impossible actions to assert independence
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist Homer logic trying to outsmart psychology

jokes.text: Oh, Daddy, you made me the happiest girl on whichever side of the Mississippi this is.
jokes.setup: Lurleen expressing happiness to her father
jokes.punchline: Geographic uncertainty despite living there
jokes.explanation: Mild joke about geographic confusion fitting with her character. Shows her simple nature.

jokes.text: Daddy's a deadbeat / He's leaving his daughter again.
jokes.punchline: Immediate reversal as he leaves again
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure - builds up reunion only to tear it down. The song format makes it more poignant.

jokes.text: Since her father re-abandoned her, she's cut the word 'pop' out of all of our foods.
jokes.punchline: Removing 'pop' from food labels as emotional response
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd way to show depression. The specificity of removing 'pop' is both sad and funny.

jokes.text: You'd think a house full of crazy people would be fun. It's actually really depressing.
jokes.punchline: Deflating expectations about crazy households
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor with Lisa's mature perspective. Subverts expectations about wacky households being entertaining.

jokes.text: Here's a new song that's rising faster than a rocket with a rocket up its butt.
jokes.punchline: Absurd double-rocket metaphor
jokes.explanation: Mildly absurd radio DJ hyperbole. The double rocket concept is silly but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: You're married to a kind, thoughtful genius. / Uh, yeah... / Marge, my fringes are knotted again.
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately proving otherwise with trivial complaint
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and irony - Homer immediately contradicts the 'genius' assessment. Marge's hesitation adds to it.

jokes.text: There's only one person in the world who can tell you what to do. And that person is right here! / Santa Claus?! / No, no, that's just our old Christmas decorations.
jokes.punchline: Lurleen misidentifies Christmas decorations as Santa
jokes.explanation: Mild visual gag undermining serious moment. The Christmas decoration confusion is appropriately silly.

jokes.text: You're not a doormat. You're a door prize!
jokes.punchline: Uplifting wordplay on door-related terms
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective wordplay that fits Marge's supportive nature. Good economy and clear structure.

jokes.text: Regular movies... don't hold your breath. But TV movies... the sky's the limit.
jokes.punchline: Brutal honesty about entertainment industry hierarchy
jokes.explanation: Good industry satire with Homer's characteristic bluntness. The distinction between regular and TV movies is accurate and funny.

jokes.text: Colonel, you're still my knight in white polyester. / Ten percent Lycra. / Oh, I noticed.
jokes.setup: Lurleen's romantic description of Homer's suit
jokes.punchline: Homer's pedantic correction about fabric content and Lurleen's appreciation
jokes.explanation: Good callback with Homer's obsession with his suit's technical specifications. Lurleen's response adds subtle flirtation.

jokes.text: I don't know. You're asking us to sell out our song. And for what? / Cash money. / Money? Ooh, we love money! / Really? That's great! / I'm gonna buy me a clock radio!
jokes.setup: Band's moral concerns about selling out
jokes.punchline: Immediate enthusiasm for money and modest purchase plan
jokes.explanation: Standard sellout joke with the clock radio being a humble purchase goal. The enthusiasm shift is well-timed.

jokes.text: Cash money. / Money? Ooh, we love money! / Really? That's great! I'm gonna buy me a clock radio!
jokes.punchline: Modest aspirations after big money talk
jokes.explanation: Good deflation from grand sellout to humble purchase

jokes.text: Okay, maybe I abandoned my daughter and stole her song, but I'm still the most honorable record producer y'all have ever met. / Well, he's got us there.
jokes.punchline: Band acknowledging he's still better than other producers
jokes.explanation: Good industry satire about low standards for record producers. The band's resigned acceptance is funny.

jokes.text: Babe, need a hundred bucks for beer. / Ooh, I think he's a keeper.
jokes.punchline: Immediately being asked for money, which she finds appealing
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lurleen's poor judgment in men. The immediate money request is predictable but well-timed.

jokes.text: Of all the women who've hit on Homer over the years, you're my favorite. / If you ever come near Homer again, I'll strangle you with your own hair extensions. That's right. I know.
jokes.punchline: Immediate threat with specific method and fashion awareness
jokes.explanation: Great Marge moment showing her protective side. The hair extension knowledge and specific threat method are perfectly crafted.
